slides.notes: Title slide. Intentional old-ledger aesthetic — cream paper, serif type, centered title, ambient ledger photograph underneath everything at 10% opacity. No clicks. Mention: invented by Venetian merchants ca. 1494, still the backbone of every ledger on Earth 500+ years later. Ask: how many modern systems survive five centuries unchanged?

elements.content: A C C O U N T I N G

elements.alt: Antique open leather-bound accounting ledger by candlelight — full-slide atmospheric background overlay

elements.content:
Every transaction has two sides.
Here's why — and how to record it.
*— as set down by merchants of the Venetian Republic, ca. 1494 —*
T E K K A L   D E M O S   ·   E D U C A T I O N

slides.notes:
Chapter One — accounting equation. On enter: CHAPTER ONE header, title, cream formula box "Assets = Liabilities + Equity" auto-reveal.

[step:1]ASSETS column reveals — "What the enterprise owns or controls: cash, inventory, equipment, receivables, prepaid expenses."[/step]

[step:2]LIABILITIES column reveals — "What the enterprise owes others: loans, accounts payable, wages, deferred revenue, bonds."[/step]

[step:3]EQUITY column reveals — "The owners' residual claim: capital contributed + retained earnings − distributions."[/step]

This identity MUST balance on every transaction. Every subsequent mechanic preserves it.

elements.content:
*What the enterprise owns or controls.*

Cash, inventory, equipment, receivables, prepaid expenses.

*What the enterprise owes others.*

Loans, accounts payable, wages owed, deferred revenue, bonds.

*The owners' residual claim.*

Capital contributed + retained earnings − distributions.

elements.alt: Traditional wooden Venetian merchant's abacus beside a parchment page and silver coins — warm candlelight still-life

slides.notes:
Chapter Two — debits, credits, T-account. On enter: Cash T-account skeleton (label, H-line, V-line, DEBIT+ / CREDIT− headers).

[step:1]Debit column entries appear in green — Jan 3 1,000 / Jan 7 500 / Jan 14 2,200.[/step]

[step:2]Credit column entries in red — Jan 5 200 / Jan 9 300 / Jan 18 1,100.[/step]

[step:3]Balance box scales in at bottom: "Balance: 2,100 DR".[/step]

[step:4]Right-side FIVE RULES panel opens — 5-row table (Assets ↑ DR, Expenses ↑ DR, Liabilities ↑ CR, Equity ↑ CR, Revenue ↑ CR) + footer note on self-audit property.[/step]

Total debits always equal total credits — that's what makes the ledger self-audit.

elements.content: *Total debits always equal total credits. This is how the ledger self-audits.*

slides.notes:
Chapter Three — worked journal entries. On enter: scenario text + 9-row journal table showing three paired debit/credit entries (Jan 1 owner contribution, Jan 15 inventory-on-account, Jan 31 rent).

[step:1]Post-check line at bottom (green): "Assets (Cash 9,200 + Inventory 2,500) = Liabilities (AP 2,500) + Equity (Capital 10,000 − Rent 800) ✓"[/step]

The invariant is preserved across every transaction — that's the whole point.

elements.content: *Scenario: In January, a small workshop opens with a $10,000 owner contribution, buys $2,500 of inventory on account, and pays $800 rent.*

slides.notes:
Chapter Four — journal → statements. On enter: pipeline auto-cascades left-to-right — Journal → Ledger → Trial Balance → Adjusting entries.

[step:1]Branching output — vertical line + arrows draw in, connecting Adjusting to three statement boxes sequentially: Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow.[/step]

Every modern reporting software traces these same steps. Only the software changes.

elements.content: *Every modern financial report traces back to these same steps — only the software changes.*

slides.notes: Epilogue. No clicks. Punchline: if the books don't balance, you haven't discovered a new kind of money — you've made an arithmetic error. Double-entry is a checksum. Fade in the punchline slowly for dramatic effect.

elements.content:
Double-entry isn't a convention — it's a checksum. When the trial balance doesn't tie out,
you haven't discovered a new kind of money. You've made an arithmetic error somewhere.
N E X T   L E C T U R E   ·   P R E P A R I N G   T H E   I N C O M E   S T A T E M E N T
